summary: Mitchell Hashimoto reflects on his career trajectory and realizes that automation has been his driving passion from the very beginning. Starting with cheating at Neopets as a kid, he progressed through automating ad clicks for money, college course registration, and eventually created Vagrant and founded HashiCorp. Each project followed the same pattern: identifying tedious manual processes and building software to eliminate them. He concludes that automation isn't just what he does—it defines who he is.
key_points: His programming journey began with a desire to automate Neopets tasks, eventually scaling to millions of virtual currency per hour through market arbitrage
Early experiments in automating real-money schemes proved lucrative but ended with legal notices to his parents
College registration automation demonstrated how software checking thousands of times per hour will always beat humans manually refreshing browsers
At Kiip, his automation obsession reached the point where he could rebuild a 50-machine production cluster from scratch in under 30 minutes
The pattern across all his work is consistent: if he had to do anything twice, he would write a program to do it instead
money_quotes: I started programming because I wanted to cheat Neopets.
I found more things to automate, realizing that computers can do tasks much more efficiently than humans, thus unlocking opportunities that didn't exist before.
At this point, I wasn't particularly interested in Neopets anymore. The automation had taken hold within me, and I was just seeing how far I could push it.
While my friends were spending hours every day refreshing course schedules hoping to get into a full class, I was just waiting for a text message.
A human refreshing a browser can't beat my software that was checking thousands of times per hour. Automation wins again.
My obsession with automation reached a point where if I had to do anything twice, I would write a program to do it for me.
Automation defines who I am, and always has.
key_insight: The most successful technologists often discover their defining obsession early—for Hashimoto, every project from Neopets bots to HashiCorp followed the same core drive: eliminating manual work through software.
